chunk_summary: Growth failure and protein-energy wasting (PEW) in pediatric CKD result from reduced intake, increased catabolism, endocrine dysfunction, and treatment-related nutrient losses, requiring early nutritional intervention and management of metabolic acidosis and dialysis-related losses.
learning_objectives: Understand the multifactorial causes of growth failure and PEW in pediatric CKD, including reduced caloric intake and metabolic acidosis.
Learn the importance of energy-protein balance and early nutritional interventions in managing pediatric CKD.
Recognize the impact of dialysis-related nutrient losses and strategies to mitigate them.
Explore the role of endocrine dysfunction in growth failure and the need for nephrology-endocrine collaboration.
key_facts: Growth failure in pediatric CKD involves reduced caloric intake, increased catabolism, and endocrine resistance.
Low energy intake leads to protein oxidation for energy, worsening anorexia and urea generation.
Metabolic acidosis promotes muscle proteolysis and reduces protein synthesis; correcting acidosis improves protein utilization.
Dialysis causes nutrient losses: PD leads to protein loss in dialysate, while HD removes amino acids and water-soluble vitamins.
Early nutritional densification and enteral support are safer than late escalation in pediatric CKD management.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Persistent low bicarbonate promotes muscle proteolysis and reduces protein synthesis; treat acidosis as a nutrition diagnosis because correction improves the efficiency with which dietary protein becomes lean tissue rather than urea.
document_key_topics: Mechanisms of growth failure in pediatric CKD
Impact of metabolic acidosis and dialysis on nutrient balance
Endocrine dysfunction and delayed puberty in CKD
